The video shows a severely clam-shelled mesh with a very thin peritoneum overlying it. The surgeon decides not to remove the mesh to avoid completely removing the peritoneum and compromising the closure of the peritoneal flap.

The surgeon begins dissection laterally, aiming to find a safe plane on the ventral side of the mesh due to the thin peritoneum overlay. This approach minimizes the risk of injuring critical structures.
The surgeon observes the mesh, which appears thick and rolled up within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins by locating the inferior epigastric vessels, which are crucial to avoid during the dissection of the mesh in this redo inguinal hernia repair.

If I can maintain hemostasis, I can see better and I can avoid the critical structures.
The surgeon approaches the inferior epigastric vessels, hugging the thick, rolled-up mesh while using cautery liberally. The sliding hernia is visible, with the colon positioned posteriorly.
The surgeon observes that the mesh is positioned away from the colon, ensuring no direct contact between the mesh and the colonic tissue.
The surgeon uses cautery along the posterior edge of the mesh, ensuring precise dissection while maintaining hemostasis.

The surgeon identifies areas with minimal scar tissue near the midline, across the midline, deep within the retropubic space, and posterior laterally over the psoas area, utilizing avascular planes for dissection.
The surgeon navigates into the midline suprapubic area, revealing minimal mesh presence. The dissection progresses from the midline towards the mesh, exposing the underlying tissue.
The surgeon uses cautery liberally in the area medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, progressing deeper into a cavity with minimal risk of damaging surrounding structures.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis within a deep pocket to prevent blood pooling, then proceeds to remove more mesh from the inferior epigastric area, having previously taken it down laterally and medially.
The surgeon navigates through a dense area of tissue, closely following the mesh to avoid damaging the inferior epigastric vessels, while using the left hand to stabilize and guide the dissection.
The surgeon places tension on the tissue intended for division, using scissors to score and separate it, ensuring that any vessels within the area are sealed before separation.

The surgeon identifies the pubis and Cooper's ligament, noting the presence of bony structures. In a hemostatic field, subtle tissue movements reveal areolar tissue adhered to the pubis, which the surgeon carefully snips close to the periosteum.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the sac, avoiding penetration, while approaching the area where the inferior epigastric vessels are adhered to the mesh. The dissection moves laterally to identify an avascular plane, with the surgeon noting the proximity to the peritoneum.
The surgeon encounters the transversus abdominis muscle, indicating that the dissection is too superficial.

The surgeon begins working towards the perineum or hernia sac, examining the thickness of the tissue being divided. A movable layer is identified, allowing the surgeon to proceed deeper to find the plane where the peritoneum can separate from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon identifies an avascular plane that appears unscarred, indicating minimal previous dissection. The anesthesiologist administers ICG, and the fibrogastric vessels are observed lighting up under fluorescence imaging.
The surgical field shows a broad area illuminated in green, with one streak of bright green likely indicating the inferior gastric artery or vein.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to activate electrocautery on the mesh, applying tension to separate tissue adhered to the mesh. The absence of visible blood vessels suggests a safe distance from the inferior epigastric vessels. The image is temporarily obscured, requiring cleaning.

The surgeon identifies the colon as part of the hernia sac and uses cautery on the anterior portion of the sac, which is devoid of colon. The surgeon carefully pulls the sac from the internal ring while using the left hand to protect the colon and lifts the tissue with scissors.

The surgeon uses their left hand to gently push the bowel away from the tissue they intend to divide, using scissors to lift the tissue. The proximity of the bowel requires careful, slow movements to avoid injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects into the retropubic space, focusing on maintaining hemostasis. The areolar tissue is visible, and the surgeon distinguishes between the bladder and surrounding tissues, noting the proximity of the bladder.

The surgeon carefully navigates the area with a millimeter or two of areolar tissue between the bladder and the periosteum, using quick, precise bursts of cautery to dissect while managing bleeding from small vessels.
The surgeon makes small movements with the cautery device, resulting in minor bleeding that is expected to coagulate naturally over time.

The surgeon uses cautery to coagulate tissue, making larger movements in an area without critical vessels. The mesh is visible between the bladder and the cauterized tissue, providing protection against bladder injury. The bleeding from earlier has stopped.

The surgeon identifies a new area of non-bloody tissue using bipolar cautery, which provides focused energy. The blue material visible is initially mistaken for Ethicon mesh, known for its blue woven threads.

The surgeon identifies a proline suture used to anchor the three max within the retropubic space, proceeding with dissection approximately two centimeters deep using Unscar technique, transitioning from known to unknown tissue areas.

The surgeon carefully scores tissue near the mesh, using visual cues to identify the mesh location. The scissors visibly bounce off the mesh as the surgeon works towards it, taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon anticipates potential bleeding while visually identifying the end of the hernia sac. The left hand is prepared to apply pressure to any bleeding vessel. The surgeon plans to dissect laterally to the inferior epigastric vessels, aiming to separate the hernia sac from the cord structures.

The surgeon carefully exposes the tissue, pulling it out to about the nine o'clock position to thin it out and locate the interface between the hernia sac and the cord structures, while remaining anterior to avoid the vas deferens.
The surgeon attempts to locate the edge of the hernia sac, but the boundary remains indistinct, leading to the creation of a large hole in the sac during the exploration.

The surgeon uses the elbow of the instrument to press down while the tip pulls away from the mesh, revealing the hernia sac. The expected edge of the sac is indicated, and the surgeon considers the potential use of a fourth arm or assistant for retraction.

The surgeon carefully dissects the inferior epigastric vessels from the mesh, focusing on clearing the medial and lateral areas before addressing the intimate connection between the vessels and the mesh. The dissection is performed with precision to avoid injury to the iliac vessels, while maintaining close proximity to the mesh.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the area near the corona mortis, where large vessels may branch from the iliac arteries. The surgeon pauses to assess the situation before proceeding laterally to avoid potential risks.

The surgeon has created a large hole in the hernia sac, aiding in the dissection by confirming the absence of bowel proximity. The surgeon examines the lateral piece, initially suspected to be testicular vessels, but upon comparison between intra-abdominal and preperitoneal views, it appears to be excessive connective tissue associated with a sliding hernia.

The surgeon identifies connective tissue that initially appeared to be the hernia sac, using a hole for visual assessment. The colon is visible nearby, requiring careful manipulation. The surgeon aims to expose the psoas muscle and posterior lateral space to ensure adequate mesh overlap for preventing recurrence.

The surgeon uses a forced bipolar instrument to grasp and retract the mesh, demonstrating its strong grip. The fenestrated bipolar mode is employed for handling bowel tissue, providing a gentler grip suitable for delicate structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mesh from the inferior epigastric vessels, iliacs, and vas deferens, using cauterization on the mesh and scraping tissue away to separate these structures.

The surgeon carefully removes the mesh from the inferior epigastric area, using cauterization on the mesh side and pushing the tissue away. The left hand provides retraction, allowing the tissue to separate from the mesh. A layer of scar tissue is visible between the mesh and the underlying critical structures.

The surgeon carefully removes tissue resembling a capsule from the mesh, maintaining a safe distance from critical structures. The mesh is visible as the tissue is peeled away, revealing underlying layers.

The surgeon creates a separation between the mesh and underlying critical structures, revealing reassuring tissue space. The vas deferens is not yet visible, but the surgeon is confident in safely detaching it without injury.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the obturator vessels and coronamortis, avoiding potential injury to the obturator nerve. The mesh is nearly detached, with visible tissue allowing safe dissection of the remaining attachments.

The surgeon navigates through a large sliding hernia, encountering excess connective tissue that complicates the dissection. The focus remains on maintaining good visualization and hemostasis while staying on the mesh to find the correct dissection plane.
The surgeon observes the edge of the mesh, noting its appearance consistent with the 3D Max mesh, while being cautious of potential energy transmission to the obturator nerve.
The surgeon approaches the obturator space within the retropubic area, noting the absence of a discrete peritoneal edge due to the presence of a sliding hernia component involving the colon.

The surgeon encounters a discrete sac edge, complicating the dissection as they differentiate between colonic tissue and cord structures. The surgeon carefully navigates this final area of dissection, attempting to identify a clear tissue plane.
The surgeon carefully pulls the bladder and mesh away from the deep retropubic space, aiming to transition the mesh onto the psoas muscle for better coverage.

The surgeon completes the dissection around the vas deferens, ensuring the mesh covers the area without interference from the bladder or peritoneum. The medial umbilical ligament is visible, but not divided due to sufficient exposure. The dissection reaches at least two centimeters deep into the retropubic space, posteriorly over the psoas muscle, providing ample coverage.

The surgeon has extended the dissection past the midline, ensuring good cephalic and lateral coverage with the new mesh. The old mesh is visible and well integrated, making removal difficult. A hole in the perineum is identified, which the surgeon plans to close using a 3-0 V-Lock 180 suture.

The surgeon identifies and separates retroperitoneal fat from the testicular vessels, ensuring that the mesh can be positioned directly on the vessels without any fat beneath it. The retroperitoneal fat is visible as a distinct tongue, and the surgeon carefully dissects to prevent it from becoming a lead point for a recurrent hernia.

The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical location of the nerves, including the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve, the femoral branch, and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, all protected by their investing fascia. The psoas stripe is visible, and the vas deferens is identified. The surgeon ensures there is sufficient space for the mesh to be placed over these structures, with the investing fascia providing a protective layer between the mesh and the nerves.
The surgeon confirms that the testicular vessels are intact and prepares to tuck the corner of the mesh into place, ensuring adequate coverage and positioning.

The surgeon uses progrip fixation to secure the posterior edge of the mesh, preventing it from lifting. The mesh is folded in a manner different from the surgeon's usual technique, as performed by the fellow.
The surgeon uses a robotic system to unfold the mesh, which was initially folded with the grip on grip, making it challenging to unfold. The seam of the mesh is demonstrated in the surgical field.

The surgeon places the seam of the mesh approximately two centimeters behind the internal ring, ensuring that the lower flap and mesh provide good posterior coverage. The mesh extends deep into the retropubic space, covering the femoral space, and laterally overlaps at least five centimeters beyond the internal ring.
The surgeon unravels the mesh, ensuring that the bladder and any fatty tissue from the hernia cannot protrude into the surgical area.

The surgeon positions a 16x12 piece of anatomical mesh, ensuring adequate overlap in all directions. The mesh is tucked down to prevent hernia fat from protruding underneath. The mesh is placed before completing dissection on the right side, as the fellow will take over the procedure.
